topic: Part 4 opening italic transition — post-ISS-963 patch re-review
change_summary: Decider patch 2026-04-14T07-28 applied both c1 recommendations to the Part 4 italic transition: (a) replaced 'Part 3 showed that individual claims fail under scrutiny' with 'Part 3 evaluated every WIN individually and found that none survive as an independent dome-only confirmation — the verdicts split between self-contradiction, refutation, standard-physics explanation, and unfalsifiable framing'; (b) appended 'and, independently, whether the framework's own statistical claim holds up when the shared inputs are audited' to add the lambda_g independent-force framing. Closes ISS-963. This review attacks the new text.
our_argument_summary: The patched intro now reads: 'Part 3 evaluated every WIN individually and found that none survive as an independent dome-only confirmation — the verdicts split between self-contradiction, refutation, standard-physics explanation, and unfalsifiable framing. This part tests whether the model's aggregate framework—its statistical convergence dashboard and site architecture—compensates for those individual weaknesses — and, independently, whether the framework's own statistical claim holds up when the shared inputs are audited.' It is the navigational bridge from Part 3 (point-by-point WIN review) to Part 4 (Live Power Dashboard & Site Page Analysis), and was revised to address two c1 moderate findings.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: N/A — this is a prose transition, not a code-bound claim.

holes_found.description: The new enumeration of Part 3 verdicts is INCOMPLETE and omits the largest category. The patched text says verdicts 'split between self-contradiction, refutation, standard-physics explanation, and unfalsifiable framing' — four categories. But the actual Part 3 verdict tally (computed from data/wins.json, 70 WINs) uses SIX categories: Self-Contradicted 14, Refuted by Data 9, Misleading 22, Std Model Explains 17, Not Demonstrated 4, Unfalsifiable 4. The enumeration covers 44/70 WINs (63%) and omits Misleading (22 = 31%, the single LARGEST category) and Not Demonstrated (4). A dome defender can (correctly) point out: 'The reviewer's own Part 3 shows 22 WINs labelled Misleading — the biggest bucket — but Part 4 summarizes Part 3 without acknowledging it. Either the enumeration should be complete or should be phrased as "including," not "split between."' This is the same failure mode the patch was meant to fix (collapsing heterogeneous verdicts into a cleaner rhetorical bucket) — it has been reduced in scope but not eliminated. 'Split between A, B, C, D' implies a partition. Omitting the largest category, specifically, will read as selective framing.
holes_found.recommendation: Option A (complete, preferred): 'the verdicts span self-contradiction, refutation, misleading framing, standard-physics duplication, unexamined claims, and unfalsifiable framing.' Option B (fuzzy signal): replace 'split between' with 'including' so the list reads as non-exhaustive. Option C (tighter, cleanest): drop the enumeration and say 'the verdicts cluster across six distinct failure modes — none of them a dome-only confirmation.' The Part 3 3.1 Verdict Tally already lists all six, so Option C offloads the detail to the authoritative source.

holes_found.description: 'None survive as an independent dome-only confirmation' uses the phrase 'dome-only' without definition at the point of first use. A reader arriving at Part 4 via direct link (the common case for a tab-based site) has not yet seen Part 2b (structural tags) or Part 3 intro (verdict categories). The phrase is rhetorically doing work — it silently presupposes the criterion that disqualifies 'Std Model Explains' verdicts (17 WINs / 24%) from counting as confirmations even though the dome's numerical prediction matched the measurement. A smart dome defender replies: 'In what sense is a correct numerical prediction not a confirmation? You have defined "confirmation" to exclude cases where we agree with standard physics, but you did not announce that definition.' This is the same defender move the c1 review flagged for 'fail under scrutiny' — it has been narrowed (from all verdicts to a specific phrase) but not closed. The intro either needs to define the term parenthetically or link/anchor to where it is defined.
holes_found.recommendation: Insert a parenthetical definition on first use: 'none survive as an independent dome-only confirmation (i.e., a numerical match that requires dome geometry and is not already explained by standard physics)'. Alternatively, hyperlink 'dome-only confirmation' to the Part 2b/3.1a definition. Cheap; closes the rhetorical opening; no length penalty worth speaking of.

holes_found.description: The body paragraph immediately below the patched italic intro is untouched and contains stale version claims that undermine the same argument. It begins 'V51.0 introduces several new site pages: a "Live Power" convergence dashboard, a "Kill-Shot" binary test page, an "Audit Walkthrough" for AI models, and a "Tracking" page.' But raw-text/08-live-power.txt shows the page's own AUDIT STATISTICS box says V51.2, not V51.0 (the page title says V51.0, the audit stats box says V51.2 — a version mismatch the dome itself hasn't reconciled). We only have direct raw-text evidence that Live Power is V51.0; the other three pages (Kill-Shot, Audit Walkthrough, Tracking) are attributed to V51.0 without supporting extracts in raw-text/. The dome's Evolution page (raw-text/06-evolution.txt) doesn't even mention V51 — it stops at V50.6/V12. A defender can attack: (a) the page is now V51.2, so 'V51.0 introduces' is out of date; (b) we can't substantiate that all four pages were introduced in V51.0 specifically without archival evidence; (c) the Evolution page doesn't list V51 at all, so our framing is sourced from a single audited page. This is adjacent to the patched intro and should be considered in the same review pass because it sits inside the same visual block.
holes_found.recommendation: Change 'V51.0 introduces several new site pages' to 'The dome site's V51 series introduces several new pages' or 'The current V51.x dome site presents several new pages (Live Power: V51.0 title, V51.2 audit stats — the dome's own version marker is inconsistent)'. Or, tightest fix: drop the version label entirely and say 'The dome site presents several new evidentiary pages...'. Out of the literal scope of the ISS-963 patch, but directly adjacent and affects the same reader's first impression of Part 4.

holes_found.description: The revised sentence is long and contains three em-dashes in close succession: 'whether the model's aggregate framework—its statistical convergence dashboard and site architecture—compensates for those individual weaknesses — and, independently, whether the framework's own statistical claim holds up when the shared inputs are audited.' The inner em-dashes (no spaces) delimit an appositive, the outer em-dashes (spaced) perform a different rhetorical function. The visual density is moderate; a skimming reader may not parse the 'independently' clause as a distinct second test. Not a defender-exploitable hole, but weakens the rhetorical impact of the new lambda_g independence framing — which was the whole point of adding the clause.
holes_found.recommendation: Split into two sentences: 'This part tests whether the model's aggregate framework — its statistical convergence dashboard and site architecture — compensates for those individual weaknesses. Independently, it tests whether the framework's own statistical claim holds up when the shared inputs are audited.' The second sentence now carries the lambda_g-independence beat cleanly and the em-dash count drops from three to two.

holes_found.description: 'Site architecture' vs 'site-page architecture' inconsistency (flagged in c1) was not addressed in the patch. The Part 3 roadmap box (SEC-1-roadmap territory) says Part 4 examines 'the Live Power dashboard, statistical convergence claims, and site-page architecture,' while the Part 4 intro still says 'site architecture.' A pedantic defender could note that Part 4 analyzes four specific pages as evidentiary artifacts, not the site's overall structural architecture; the Part 3 wording ('site-page architecture') is more accurate. The section heading itself is 'Live Power Dashboard & Site Page Analysis,' which again aligns with 'site-page' rather than 'site architecture.'
holes_found.recommendation: Change 'site architecture' to 'site-page architecture' in the italic intro. Matches Part 3 roadmap, matches Part 4's own section heading, and matches what Part 4 actually does. Truly trivial edit.

stronger_arguments.description: The intro's independent-force clause ('whether the framework's own statistical claim holds up when the shared inputs are audited') could be sharpened by naming the stake: '14 of 20 domains share the same fitted constant lambda_g = 8,619 km — the convergence collapses if Fisher-independence fails.' This previews Part 4.1's core argument and makes the 'independently' framing concrete rather than abstract.
sources: data/sections.json part4 (body paragraph already cites 14 of 20 and lambda_g explicitly)

stronger_arguments.description: Consider mirroring the three-part structure the Part 3 roadmap now uses ('individual claims → aggregate methodology → self-imposed falsification'). The Part 4 intro could say 'This is the middle tier of the three-part attack: if individual WINs are in doubt and the aggregate framework fails its independence test, Part 5 will then apply the dome's own falsification rule.' This positions Part 4 in the cross-part logic explicitly. May be overkill for a transitional italic, but currently Part 4 feels orphaned from Parts 3 and 5 unless the reader also reads the Part 3 roadmap box.

kernel_of_truth.description: The patch correctly addressed both c1 moderate findings. 'Fail under scrutiny' is gone; the lambda_g independent-force clause is present. The analyst/decider responded to adversarial feedback exactly as intended — this is the pipeline working. The remaining issues (enumeration incompleteness, undefined 'dome-only') are smaller and second-order. The intro is genuinely better than it was yesterday.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The patch reduced the scope of the framing problem but did not eliminate it. Swapping a fuzzy collective verb ('fail under scrutiny') for an explicit enumeration raises the evidentiary bar — the enumeration must now be accurate and complete, and it is not. Defender rhetoric migrates from 'you're calling Std-Model-Explains a failure' to 'you're hiding Misleading' — a smaller attack surface, but still non-zero.

text_fingerprint.transition_text_excerpt: Part 3 evaluated every WIN individually and found that none survive as an independent dome-only confirmation — the verdicts split between self-contradiction, refutation, standard-physics explanation, and unfalsifiable framing. This part tests whether the model's aggregate framework—its statistical convergence dashboard and site architecture—compensates for those individual weaknesses — and, independently, whether the framework's own statistical claim holds up when the shared inputs are audited.
text_fingerprint.location:
data/sections.json part4 html, first

after
text_fingerprint.adjacent_body_first_clause: V51.0 introduces several new site pages

deeper_analysis:
Considering both sides per user preference:

Attack side (the adversarial case): The enumeration omission is the single clearest remaining hole. It is also a foreseeable pattern — whenever we swap a fuzzy collective description ('fail under scrutiny') for a specific enumeration ('split between A, B, C, D'), we incur an obligation to be complete. The patch did not audit the enumeration against data/wins.json. The fact that the LARGEST category (Misleading, 22 WINs) is the one omitted looks suspicious whether or not it was deliberate — a dome defender who checks the Part 3 tally will notice immediately. The Not Demonstrated omission (only 4 WINs) is a smaller point but adds up with Misleading.

Defender side (what the current text gets right): A reader who accepts the enumeration as illustrative rather than exhaustive will read it as signalling four failure *kinds* rather than listing every verdict value. In that frame, 'Misleading' and 'Not Demonstrated' are sub-types that roll up into the named kinds (Misleading ≈ a form of Refutation-lite; Not Demonstrated ≈ a form of unfalsifiable). If you argue for that reading, the enumeration is not wrong, just coarse. The counter to that counter is: the Part 3 Verdict Tally treats the six categories as distinct, and so should the Part 4 summary. Consistency with Part 3 is a hard constraint; fuzziness in how Part 4 echoes Part 3 is an error in the aggregate.

On the 'dome-only confirmation' point: the pro-current reading is that 'dome-only' is a deliberate dog-whistle signalling the uniqueness filter that the whole review rests on, and the reader will catch the meaning in context. The con reading is that a tab-based site cannot rely on top-down reading order — a defender who links directly to the Part 4 tab will hit this undefined term first. Given the rest of the review's care to define uniqueness explicitly (Part 2b structural tags, Part 3.1a analytical tags), leaving it undefined here is a gap.

On the adjacent V51.0 paragraph: strictly outside the patch scope, but directly adjacent and it would be odd to review the italic without noticing the version inconsistency three lines down. The dome's own site uses V51.0 (title) and V51.2 (audit stats) on the same page, and the Evolution page still stops at V50.6 — our framing picks one and runs with it, which a defender can exploit by pointing to the other. Flagging it here makes the decider's next triage easier.

No DOIs, URLs, or cite-able sources in the transition itself. No quantitative claims in the italic. All risk is rhetorical and categorical.
verdict_summary: The ISS-963 patch is substantively successful — both c1 moderate findings were addressed, and the intro is measurably better. One MAJOR hole remains: the new four-category enumeration omits Misleading (22 WINs, the largest single verdict bucket) and Not Demonstrated (4 WINs), covering only 44/70 WINs (63%). Two MODERATE holes: 'dome-only confirmation' is undefined at first use (rhetorically loads 'Std Model Explains' cases without acknowledging the presupposition), and the adjacent un-patched body paragraph ('V51.0 introduces...') carries a stale version label (dome's own page now shows V51.2 in audit stats). Two MINOR holes (doubled em-dash sentence; 'site architecture' vs 'site-page architecture' inconsistency with Part 3 roadmap). Recommended: a follow-up patch either completes the enumeration or softens 'split between' to 'including,' plus a parenthetical definition of 'dome-only confirmation.' No verdict change — the intro's overall argument stands.
